California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
Uay 3, 1990
Lyle L. Lopus
Assistant city Attorney
c j"ty Attorney's office
39700 civic center Drive
P. O. Box 5005
Frenont, cA 94537
Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
our FiIe No. I-9 o-2 og
Dear Mr. Lopus:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regard-
ing the duties and responsibil ities of Mayor Bill Ball and Plan-
ning Conmissioner Pauline Mclvor under the conflict-of-interest
p.oii=iorr= of the Political Reform Act (the nag6rr1.1 we do not
have sufficient facts to provj.de a definite answer to your ques-
tions; therefore, we consider your letter to be a request for
informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy
enclosed) . 2
OUESTIONS
1. Do the conf lict-of-j.nterest provisions of the Act
disqualify llayor BalI from participating in decisions related to
the adoption of a specific plan for the Centerville district?
2. Do the confl ict-of-interest provisions of the Act
disqualify Conrnissioner llcIvor fron participating in decisions
related to the adoption of a specj.f ic plan for the Centerville
district?
CONCLUS ION
1. The conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act disqualj.fy
l,[ayor Ball fron participating in decisions related to the adopti.on
of a specific plan for the centerville district if the decisions
1' 
Government code Sections 81000-91015. AI1 statutory references
are to the Governnent Code unless otherwise indicated. Conrnission
regulations appear at 2 california code of Regulations . section
18OOO, et seq. AII references to regulations are to Titl-e 2,
Division 6 of the california code of Regulations.
provide the requestor with the
or fornal written advice. (Section
2 Inforrnal assistance does not
imrnunity provided bY an oPinion
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )

wil,l have a material financial effect on the value of his
residence.
2. The conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act disqualify
Coromissioner McIvor from participating in decisions related to the
adoption of a specific plan for the Centerville district 
because
her real property interests are directly affected by the deci-
s ions .
rACTS
The city of Fremont is in the process of developing a
specific plan for the centerville district. 
The centerville
district is one of the original five towns which jointly
incorporated in 1956 to form the City of Fremont. Mayor Ball and
his wife own and reside in a single-fanily residence in the
centerville district. 
Planning Cornmissioner McIvor and her
husband or.rn two single-farnily residences in the centervil-Ie
district, 
both of which they rent to residential tenants. These
three homes are in estabLished residential areas where land use
changes are not contenplated. llayor Ball's home is within the
survey area but outside the study area in which najor land use
changes are being proposed. Coromissioner llclvor's rental homes
are within the study area:
The centervill,e district has experienced slo!, economic Atrowth
for a number of years due to the location of most new retaj,J.,
service, and office facilities 
in other districts and in nearby
shopping ma11s. The decline of the area has been ninirnized due to
the success of several new car dealerships which dornj.nate
centerville's commercial area. llost of these new car deal,ers have
expressed interest in relocating to an auto naIl to be built away
from the centerville district 
and on the west side of highway
I-880 in the city of Fremont.
The City of Fremont has a population of approxinately
170,000. The populatj.on of the CentervilLe district is estirnated
at 38,875 residents.3 Additionally, out of a total of 15,92L
developed acres in the city, the Centerville district has ap-
proxinately 3,125 acres. The city has approximately 42,000 single
fanily residences, 10,500 of which are located in the centerville
district. 
of these 10,500 units, approxinately 700 are in the
survey area where llayor Ball's residence is located. In the study
areas there are more than 300 residences rhich are generally
sirnilar to those ovned by l,[ayor BaIl and Conmissioner ]lclvor.
These fig'ures indicate that the centerville district, 
r.rhich is one
of eight planning areas in the city, courprlses fron twenty to
twenty five pe-cent of the city in tenns of single fanily
residences, developed acreage, and population. Hoirever, the
' city of frenont Assistant Planner Janice Stern provided us with
these iigures during a telephone conversation on April 2, 1990.


Fi■e No. エー90-208
Page 3
specific pLan would only apply to the study area $/hich contains a
small fraction of the total nurnber of homes in the CentervilLe
district.
The city designed a Centervj-l1e survey area consisting of
approxinately 40 square blocks plus frontage along three niles of
Fremont Boulevard, along about one uile of Thornton Avenue, and
along one and one-third niles of Central Avenue. A study area was
Iater defined vhich encompasses about eighty percent of the survey
area. The study area and the survey area are both part of the
Centerville planning area. The conmercial core of centerville is
surrounded by single family hornes and apartments.
The nain objectives of the Centerville specif j,c plan are (1)
to prepare a land use plan for reuse of the auto dealer sj.tes and
other vacant and underutilized land, and (2) to adopt strategies
for economic revitalization of the area. You state in your
request for advice that although successful revitalization of the
Centervil,l,e business district would have a salutary effect on the
lrhoIe Centerville planning area, actual specific plan land use
changes are likely to be linited to parcels rneeting one or nore of
the following criteria:
1. Land and/or buildings currently vacant;
2. Auto dealer or auto-related use;
3. Underutilized land where the built space is significantly
Iess than allosred by zoning; and
4. Obsolete design/ significant lack of maintenance and
investment.
These criteria have been applied principally in the comrnercial
business district. 
In general, there have been no land use
chanqes reconunended in any established residential neighborhood in
the Centerville district.
It is your understanding that the planning rrork completed so
far (three concept plans) suggests that, lrhi1e certain aspects of
the proposed plans may operate to increase the value and rental
value of the BaIl and llclvor properties, inplementation of the
plans would have a substantially sinilar effect on numerous other
residential properties in and near the survey and study areas and
probably, but to a far lesser degree, throughout the entire plan-
ning area.
ANALYSI S
Section 87100 prohibits any public official 
from rnaking,
participating in naking, or othenrise using his or her official
position to j.nfluence a governmental decision in which the of-
ficial has a financial interest. 
Section 87103 specifies that an
official 
has a financial interest within the meaning of Section


File No. I-9 O-2 0I
Page 4
87100 if j.t is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public Aenerally, on the official or a nember of his or her
inmediate fanily or on:
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
Section 871.03 (b) .
Mayor Ball and conmissioner llclvor are public officials.
(section 82048.) For purposes of our discussj.on we assune that
each has an interest in real property in the centerville district
which exceeds $1,000. Thus, llayor BaIl and Commissioner liIclvor
are prohibited fron naking or participating in decisions which
would have a reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on
their real property that is distinguishable from the effect on the
public aeneral Iy.
Foreseeabi l ity
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be more than a mere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not required. 
(Dolrnev cares v. Downev
communitv Developroent Com. (1987) 196 Ca1. App. 3d 983, 989-99L;
Witt v. Uorror, (f9771 70 CaI. App. 3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed) . ) The Act seeks to prevent more
than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. l,torrow,
supra at 823. )
It is reasonably foreseeable that revitalization of the
centerville business district will have a favorable impact on
property located in the study area and its innediate surroundings.
As land use is upgraded, property becones more desirable and the
value of homes increases. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable that
revitalization of the centerville business district will have a
favorable iropact on the value of l,tayor Ball,s and Conmissioner
tlclvor,s properties. Horrever, disqualification is not reqr.rired
unless the effect of decisions regarding the Centerville district
lrouId have a naterial financial effect on the public officials,
properties, and such effect is distingnrishable from the effect on
the public Aenerally.
uaterial Financial Effect
The Conmission has aqopted a serj.es of reg'ulations to
deternine whether the finaneial. effect of a decision is naterial,.
fn order to apply the regn:lations with respect to real property
interests, it is necessary to deternine the location of the
property with respect to the project area.


FiIe No. I-9 0-2 08
Page 5
Mavor Ball's residence
The city council will be adopting a specific plan establish-
ing peruritted land uses and standards of development for a portion
of the Centerville district. 
Mayor BaIl owns and resides in real
property within the survey area but outside the study area which
will be affected by the specific p1an. Thus, any effect upon
Mayor BaII,s property resulting fron adoption of the specific plan
will be indirect.
When a decision affects real property indirectly, the effect
of the decision is naterial if:
(1) The real property in which the officiaL
has an interest, or any of that real property, is
located within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries) of the property which
is the subject of the decision, unless the decision
hri11 have no financial effect upon the officialrs
real property interest.
***
' 
(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,5oo feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision wiII
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,0o0) or Dore on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official 
has an interest....
***
(e) Redevelopment Decisions: For purposes of
this section rrthe boundaries (or proposed
boundaries ) of the property which is the subject of
the decisionrt are the boundaries (or proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopnent decision to
designate the survey area, to rnake findings of
bIight, to select the project area, to adopt the
prelininary plan, to forn a project area cornmittee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment pIan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions.
Regulation L8702.3 (a) and (e), copy
enclosed.


' 
File No. I-9 O-2 08
Page 6
we have insufficient inforrnation to deterrnine the distance
between Mayor Ball's residence and the boundaries of the study
area covered by the speci,f ic plan. Horrever, if the residence j-s
within 300 feet of the boundaries of the study area, decisions
regarding the centervil-le district will be deemed to naterially
affect l{ayor Ball's property unless the decisions wiLl have no
financial effect on his property. If the distance from the
residence to the boundaries of the study area is ruore than 300
feet but less than 2,5oo feet, the effect of a decision regarding
the study area will be deemed material if it will increase or
decrease the value of the residence by $10,ooo or nore.
Cornmissioner Mclvor's residential rental units
The planning cornrnission will subnit to the city council its
recommendation for adoption of a specific plan for the Centerville
district. 
Cornmissioner Mclvor owns two residential rental units
within the area covered by the specific plan. Thus, the effect of
decisions regardj"ng the specific pfan on her real property
interests will be direct. 
when an official's 
economic interests
are directly involved in a decision, the effect is naterj.al if:
The decision is to designate the survey area,
to select the project area, to adopt the
prelirninary p1an, to form a project area cornmittee,
to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of
the above decisions; and real property in which the
official 
has an interest, or any part of it is
located within the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the redevelopnent area.
Regulation La702.1(a) (3) (D), copy
enclosed.
Adoption of a specific plan for the centerville district is akin
to decisions involving redevelopnent. Such decisions are designed
to, and do result in, inproved property values within the area.
Because Conrnissioner UcIvor,s residential rental units are located
within the study area which will be the subject of the specific
p1an, the effect of any decision regarding the specific plan will
be deemed rnaterial, and require Commissioner UcIvor,s disgualifica-
tion unless the effect is distinguishabl"e from the effect on the
public aenerally.
Public cenerallv
The renaining issue is whether adoption of a specific plan
for the Centerville district will affect the elected officials 
who
own real property in the district in a nanner that is distinguish-
able from the effect on the public generally.


File No. I-9 0-2 oB
Page 7
The Commission has adopted a regulation and several opinions
which are of assistance in determining whether a public official,s
interests wiII be affected in a manner distinguishable from those
of the public generally. Regulation 18703 (copy enclosed)
provides, in part:
A material financial effect of a governmental
decision on an official,s 
interests, as described
in Governnent Code Section 87!03, is distinguish-
able fron its effect on the public generally unless
the decision will affect the official,s 
interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect all
members of the public or a significant segment of
the publ ic.
Regulation 18703.
The "publicrr is aI1 the persons residing, owning property, or
doing business in the jurisdiction of the agency in question. (In
re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1, copy enclosed.) In the case of the
mayor and a planning commissioner, the public consi.sts of all the
residents of the city. 
consequently, for the ttpublic generallytr
exception to appl.y, a decision must affect the officials,
j,nterests in substantially the satne manner as it will affect a
significant segrment of the population of the city of Fremont.
(Dowd Advice Letter, No. A-88-214; Burnham Advice Letter, No.
A-85-21o, copies enclosed. )
The Commission has never adopted a strict arithnetic test for
determining what constitutes a sj,gnif icant segTment of the public.
However, in order to apply the public generally exception, the
population affected uust be large in nunber and heterogeneous in
nature. (In re Ferraro (1978) 4 rPPC Ops. 62; F1vnn Advice Let-
ter, No. I-88-430, copies enclosed.)
Mavor Ba1l,s residence
You correctly point out in your request for advice that the
Conmission has found that property owners residing in their
propertj.es in the downtown rrcore arearr of the City of Davj-s
constituted a significant segment of tl€ public. 
(In re Owen
(L976, 2 FPPC Ops. 77, 8]-. copy enclosed.) Based on the general
benefit the r.core arearr plan would bring to all the residents and
to the business conrnunity in the City of Davis, the Commission
concl,uded that the plan affected a significant segment of the
public in substantially the sane Danner as it affected the public
official. 
Thus, if a significant segrBent of aII honeowners in the
City of EreDont will be affected by the iroprovement of the
centervrlle district in a nanner substantially siuilar to Mayor
BaI1, the effect of decisions regarding the Centerville specific
plan on the mayor,s hone will not be considered distinguishable
fron the effect on the public aenerally.


File No. I-90-208
Page 8
Your particular set of facts indicates that the centerville
district consists of between twenty and twenty-five percent of the
city,s existing residential area and population. Houever, the
Centerville business district is not the downtown rrcore arearr of
the city. 
(see owen, supra). Therefore, the inpact of the
revitalization of the centerville district will not be felt in the
sane manner by atl the residents of the city. 
For example,
residents of districts other than centerville, while perhaps
benefiting intangibly from the inproved appearance of the
centerville business district, 
are likely to feel no impact on the
value of their homes due to the revitalization of the Centerville
district. 
whereas the project uould, j.n theory, benefit the
entire city Uy elininating bIight, property owners vithin the
study area and its inunediate proxirnity will see their property
values enhanced to a greater degree than those property o\./ners
nore distanced from the project. 
we conclude, therefore, that
owens, supra, is inapplicable to your facts and that homeowners in
the centervil.l-e district do not constitute a significant segment
of the public. Accordj,ngly, Mayor Ball must disqualify hi:nself
from participating in any decision which will have a material
financial effect on his residence.
Comrnissioner McIvor's residentiaf rental units
Corumissioner l.tclvor owns two residential rental units in the
study area. The ttpublic generall-y[ exception, as applied to com-
missioner Mcfvor's facts, nay be deemed to be such residents of
the City of Fremont lrho own less than three residential rental
uni-ts. This is so because the owners of a sma11 number of rental
units are a diverse segment of the population representing all
occupations and interests and whose only conmon bond is the owner-
ship of rental property. 
(See Ferraror SIIEEE r at paqe 66.) 
we
have insufficient information to determine the number of persons
rrho own tess than three residential rental units in the City of
Fremont. However, for purposes of our analysis, this information
is of no irnport. Any decision affecting developnent of the
centervilte district study area will have an effect on cornmis-
sioner Mclvor's interests in residential rental property located
directly within the study area. owners of residential rental
property farther removed fron the study area will not be affected
by the revitalization of the centerville district in the sane man-
ner as Comuissioner }lclvor. The number of residences within the
study area is too smalI to constitute a significant seg:ment of the
public. 
The nunber of persons uho own less than three residentiaL
rental units in this area would be even sualler and would not
constitute a significant seginent of the public. Therefore, the
public generally exception does not apply and the conmissioner
uust disqualify herself frorn participating in decisions regarding
the Centerville study area.

■-90-208
`           Page 9
We trust this 
■etter adequate■y responds to your inquiry.
Shou■d you have any further questions regarding this matter′
 do
not hesitate to ca■
■ me at (9■6) 322-590■
.
Sincerely′
Kathryn Eo Donovan
Ceneral Counsel

has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

(witt v. l,torrow,

unless the effect of decisions regarding the Centerville district

fn order to apply the regn:lations with respect to real property

property with respect to the project area.

of the Centerville district.

property within the survey area but outside the study area which
will be affected by the specific p1an. Thus, any effect upon

Mayor Ball and his wife own and reside in a single-fanily residence in the centerville district.
